54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68	
69	## Slug
70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74

76	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}
114	
115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{thm:another_result}
120	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
121	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
122	\end{proof}
123	```
124	
125	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. 141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
147	
148	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".

153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.

156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. 1	# Analogy: Mathlib API path for `Scheme.RationalMap.order`
2	
3	## Mode
4	api-alignment
5	
6	## Slug
7	dvr-rationalmap-order
8	
9	## Iteration
10	175
11	
12	## Question
13	For the body of `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order`
14	(`AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean` L140), what is the correct
15	Mathlib API path to (a) extract the discrete valuation at a codim-1 point of
16	a (locally noetherian, regular-in-codim-1) integral scheme, (b) extend that
17	valuation from the local ring to the fraction field, and (c) handle the
18	junk-on-`f = 0` convention?
19	
20	## Project artifact(s)
21	- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean`:140-142 — `RationalMap.order` body sorry.
22	- `blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex`, `def:order_at_point` block (statement-only `\leanok`).
23	- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean`:80-97 — `Scheme.PrimeDivisor`
24	  carrier (`coheight : Order.coheight point = 1`).
25	
26	## Decisions identified
27	
28	### Decision 1: Use `Ring.ordFrac` vs. roll-your-own DVR-`addVal`-then-extend
29	
30	- **Mathlib idiom**: `Ring.ordFrac R : K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` (with `ℤᵐ⁰ = WithZero (Multiplicative ℤ)`),
31	  defined at `Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic:324` (Stacks `02MD`). The
32	  hypotheses are exactly `[CommRing R] [Nontrivial R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
33	  [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]` plus `[Field K] [Algebra R K] [IsFractionRing R K]`.
34	  Internally `ordFrac` extends `Ring.ordMonoidWithZeroHom R : R →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` to the
35	  fraction field via `Submonoid.LocalizationMap.lift₀` (the `lift₀` already
36	  handles the units-mapping discharge). The integer value on `r ∈ R` is the
37	  length of `R / (r)` (= the DVR valuation for an `r`-uniformised local DVR);
38	  on `a/b ∈ K`, it is `ord(a) - ord(b)` (in `Multiplicative ℤ` terms).
39	  Why Mathlib chose it: the natural codim-1 generality covers BOTH the
40	  global-Noetherian / Krull-dim-1 case (Dedekind domains, regular curves) and
41	  the strictly local DVR case; one definition serves both — the DVR
42	  `addVal` is then derivable as a corollary, not a prerequisite. The
43	  intermediate length-of-quotient definition `Ring.ord` (Stacks 02MD) is what
44	  lets the same formula work in any Krull-dim-≤-1 ring (not just DVRs).
45	- **Project's current path**: bare `sorry` body (iter-172 file-skeleton); the
46	  iter-174 docstring comment proposes "extract DVR addVal then extend to
47	  fraction field", which is the parallel-API path.
48	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost (project's proposed path is parallel API; the
49	  shipped body has nothing yet, so the cost is *avoidance* of the parallel
50	  build, not refactor of shipped code).
51	- **Cost of divergence (if Project goes its own way)**:
52	  - 4–6 bridge lemmas to materialise an `AddValuation` on the stalk and lift
53	    to the fraction field (`IsDiscreteValuationRing.addVal` produces
54	    `AddValuation R ℕ∞`; `AddValuation.ofValuation` round-trips through
55	    `Valuation R (Multiplicative ℕ∞ᵒᵈ)`; `Valuation.extendToLocalization`
56	    needs the supp-disjoint side condition; finally the `ℕ∞ → ℤ` cast adds
57	    a junk choice on `⊤`). Each step is a separate definition and proof.
58	  - Downstream: `principal` (`Mathlib`-style `K → Div(X)` Finsupp) cannot
59	    consume `IsDedekindDomain.HeightOneSpectrum.valuation`-style adic
60	    valuations directly; it gets a project-bespoke `addVal` that must be
61	    bridged again at every Hartshorne-style identity (`v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) +
62	    v_Y(g)`, etc.).
63	  - Loses access to `Ring.ord_mul`, `ord_pow`, `ord_of_isUnit`,
64	    `ordMonoidWithZeroHom_eq_ord`, `ordFrac_eq_div` — every algebraic
65	    identity needed for `thm:principal_hom` would be re-proved.
66	- **Verdict**: **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB** — the body must use `Ring.ordFrac`.
67	
68	### Decision 2: Junk-on-`f = 0` convention (`ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ` extraction)
69	
70	- **Mathlib idiom**: `WithZero.log : ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ` from
71	  `Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero:394`, defined as
72	  `x.recZeroCoe 0 Multiplicative.toAdd`. Properties:
73	  - `log_zero : log 0 = 0` (the junk-on-zero convention)
74	  - `log_exp : log (exp a) = a` (round-trip on nonzero)
75	  - `log_mul : log (x * y) = log x + log y` for `x, y ≠ 0` (the additive
76	    valuation identity that `thm:principal_hom` will downstream consume).
77	  This is the *named* Mathlib idiom for converting `ℤᵐ⁰` (the multiplicative
78	  value group with junk-zero) to the additive `ℤ` value group with junk-zero.
79	  The dual `WithTop ℤ → ℤ` is `WithTop.untop₀` (used by
80	  `MeromorphicOn.divisor` at `Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor`); for our
81	  multiplicative-value-group case `WithZero.log` is the direct match.
82	  Why Mathlib chose it: it makes the junk choice canonical and reusable, so
83	  every downstream identity (e.g. `log_mul`, `log_pow`) lands as a `simp`
84	  lemma rather than a per-call-site case-split.
85	- **Project's current path**: not chosen yet (statement-only).
86	- **Gap**: identical (no prior project pin to compare).
87	- **Verdict**: **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB** — use `WithZero.log`.
88	
89	### Decision 3: DVR-from-codim-1 chain (closing the typeclass synthesis)
90	
91	- **Mathlib idiom**:
92	  - `AlgebraicGeometry.instIsNoetherianRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsLocallyNoetherian`
93	    (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian`): for `[IsLocallyNoetherian X]`,
94	    the stalk is `IsNoetherianRing`.
95	  - `AlgebraicGeometry.instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral`
96	    (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField`): for `[IsIntegral X]`, every
97	    stalk is `IsDomain` (hence `Nontrivial`).
98	  - `AlgebraicGeometry.instIsFractionRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafFunctionField`
99	    (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField`): for `[IsIntegral X]`, the
100	    function field is a fraction ring of the stalk at every point — so
101	    `[IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) X.functionField]` is
102	    automatic.
103	  - For Krull-dim-1: `IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim_eq_height`
104	    (`Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height`) + `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_stalk`
105	    (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme`) gives
106	    `ringKrullDim (stalk) = primeIdealOf(x).height`.
107	- **Project's current path**: the chapter pre-pins (iter-173 prose, blueprint
108	  L98–L112) the project-side conjunction
109	  `∀ x, Order.coheight x = 1 → IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk x)`,
110	  not yet bundled as a typeclass.
111	- **Gap**: divergent-and-incomplete — **Mathlib has no direct
112	  `Order.coheight x = 1 → Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk x)` bridge**.
113	  The missing fact is the topological identity "coheight of `x` in the
114	  scheme's specialisation preorder equals the height of the prime ideal
115	  corresponding to `x` in any affine chart" (Stacks `02IZ` / `005X`). The
116	  step "ideal height → stalk Krull dim" is in Mathlib
117	  (`IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim_eq_height`); the topological-to-
118	  prime-spectrum bridge is the gap.
119	- **Cost of divergence**: low for iter-175 — the workaround is to
120	  thread `[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]` (or the explicit
121	  `[IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]` if downstream cares)
122	  as an explicit instance/hypothesis on the `order` signature at iter-175;
123	  the topological bridge can be deferred to a later iter or upstreamed to
124	  Mathlib as a small PR.
125	- **Verdict**: **NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL** for the topological coheight bridge;
126	  **PROCEED** with explicit-instance threading for iter-175.
127	
128	## Recommendation
129	
130	**Body of `RationalMap.order` (iter-175 prover lane):**
131	
132	```lean
133	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
134	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
135	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
136	    (f : X.functionField) : ℤ :=
137	  WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f)
138	```
139	
140	Rationale per decision:
141	
142	1. **`Ring.ordFrac`** is the canonical Mathlib API for "extension of the DVR
143	   `addVal` to the fraction field, in a way that respects the units-on-`R^×`
144	   and gives a `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero hom". It works for ANY Noetherian
145	   Krull-dim-≤-1 commutative ring; under the project's additional
146	   `regular-in-codim-1` standing hypothesis the same value coincides with
147	   `IsDiscreteValuationRing.addVal` cast to `ℤ` (via the chain
148	   `ordFrac_eq_ord` ⟹ `ordMonoidWithZeroHom_eq_ord` ⟹ DVR `addVal_uniformizer`).
149	2. **`WithZero.log`** is the canonical `ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ` projection with junk-on-zero;
150	   `log 0 = 0`, and `log_mul`/`log_pow` give the additive-valuation algebra
151	   identities `thm:principal_hom` will downstream consume for free.
152	3. **Typeclass threading**: add `[IsLocallyNoetherian X]` and
153	   `[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]` as instance arguments on
154	   the `order` signature. The `IsFractionRing` instance is automatic from
155	   `[IsIntegral X]`; `Nontrivial` of the stalk is automatic from
156	   `IsDomain`. The codim-1 stalk instance is the project-side workaround for
157	   the missing Mathlib topological bridge (Decision 3).
158	
159	**Downstream impact (for `principal`, `principal_hom`):**
160	
161	- `principal f hf` becomes `Finsupp.onFinset`-style construction with
162	  Hartshorne 6.1 supplying finite support; values are
163	  `order Y f = WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ f)`.
164	- `principal_hom` reduces to `log_mul` / `log_one` on the underlying `ℤᵐ⁰`
165	  values, applied prime-divisor-wise.
166	- `principal_degree_zero`'s sub-build (Hartshorne 6.10) is unchanged by this
167	  decision — the proof goes through the finite morphism into `ℙ¹` either way.
168	
169	**Blueprint-side gap closure (writer's task this plan-phase):**
170	
171	The `def:order_at_point` block should add a "Lean encoding" paragraph pinning
172	the API choice as:
173	
174	> Lean encoding: `order Y f := WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk
175	> Y.point) f)`, where `Ring.ordFrac` is Mathlib's monoid-with-zero hom
176	> `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` from `Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic` (Stacks 02MD).
177	> The junk-on-`f = 0` convention is `order Y 0 = 0`, inherited from
178	> `WithZero.log 0 = 0`. The Krull-dim-1 hypothesis on the stalk is threaded
179	> as `[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]` (the topological
180	> coheight → stalk Krull-dim bridge is Mathlib-upstream-pending; see
181	> Stacks 02IZ).
182	
183	**Mathlib upstream opportunity (optional, not iter-175 blocking):**
184	
185	A two-line PR to Mathlib pinning the bridge
186	`Order.coheight (x : (Spec R)) = (asIdeal x).height` (and the `Scheme`-level
187	corollary via `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_stalk`) would let the project
188	**drop** the explicit `[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 _]` argument and synthesise it
189	purely from `Order.coheight Y.point = 1` (via the structure carrier). This
190	is the cleanest long-term shape but is *not* on the iter-175 critical path.

1	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean
2	
3	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
7	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
8	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),
9	% 02ME (order at a point on a regular 1-dimensional scheme),
10	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
11	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
12	
13	\section{Setup and motivation}
14	
15	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
16	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
17	\[
18	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
19	  \,\colon\,
20	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
21	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
22	\]
23	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
24	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
25	Riemann--Roch dimension formula $\ell(D) = \deg D + 1$ for $\deg D \geq 0$ on
26	a genus-$0$ curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
27	$\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ via $1$ and a non-constant function
28	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve $\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1$''
29	classification recovered as a degree-$1$ morphism into $\mathbb P^1$ via
30	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
31	
32	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
33	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
34	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
35	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
36	codimension-$1$ cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
37	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
38	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
39	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
40	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
41	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
42	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
43	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
44	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
45	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
46	
47	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
48	$\Div(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \codim 1}} \mathbb Z$ on a
49	Noetherian integral scheme $X$ satisfying Hartshorne's condition $(*)$, the
50	principal-divisor homomorphism $\div \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \Div(X)$ on a
51	curve, the degree map $\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z$ on a smooth proper
52	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
53	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
54	(\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
55	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
56	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on $C$ defines a
57	finite morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1$). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
58	O_C(D)$ and the associated cohomology computation $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
59	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
60	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-$0$ classification proper lives in
61	\texttt{RR.4}.
62	
63	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
64	$(*)$:
65	
66	% SOURCE QUOTE: "$(\ast)$ $X$ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
67	% is regular in codimension one."
68	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional $(*)$.}
69	\[
70	  (\ast)\qquad
71	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
72	  codimension one.}
73	\]
74	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
75	additionally assume $X$ is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
76	an algebraically closed field $\bar k$, where Hartshorne's condition $(*)$ is
77	automatic.
78	
79	\paragraph{Standing hypothesis $(*)$ in the Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).}
80	Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) does not bundle Hartshorne's four-clause
81	$(\ast)$ as a single typeclass. The chapter pins $(*)$ to a \emph{layered}
82	typeclass set that the prover-agent threads through the Lean signatures in
83	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean}:
84	
85	\begin{itemize}
86	  \item \textbf{Basic formal-sum layer} (carrier of \Cref{def:prime_divisor} and
87	    \Cref{def:codim1_cycles}, the additive group $\Div(X)$, the degree
88	    \Cref{def:divisor_degree}, the degree-homomorphism
89	    \Cref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}, and \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}): the
90	    only Mathlib typeclass invoked is \texttt{[IsIntegral X]}
91	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} via
92	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Properties}). Integrality of $X$ already
93	    delivers \texttt{IrreducibleSpace X.carrier} (the carrier-level instance
94	    chain), so $X$ has a unique generic point and the function field
95	    \texttt{X.functionField} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField})
96	    is a field; separatedness and noetherianness are \emph{not} required for
97	    the bare formal-sum data type.
98	  \item \textbf{Order/principal layer} (\Cref{def:order_at_point},
99	    \Cref{def:principal_divisor}, \Cref{thm:principal_hom}): the Lean
100	    signature additionally threads
101	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}
102	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian}) and Hartshorne's
103	    regular-in-codimension-one clause. The latter has \emph{no} Mathlib
104	    typeclass shipping it directly; the chapter's pin is the project-side
105	    predicate ``for every $x \in X$ with $\codim_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1$ the
106	    stalk $\mathcal O_{X,x}$ is a discrete valuation ring'', encodable as
107	    the conjunction
108	    $\forall x,\; \texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1 \Rightarrow
109	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing}\,(\mathcal O_{X,x})$. Iter-173+ may
110	    introduce a \texttt{Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne} predicate to
111	    abbreviate this; until then the order/principal lemmas thread the DVR
112	    hypothesis explicitly at each codim-1 point.
113	  \item \textbf{Curve layer} (\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}): the standing
114	    hypothesis tightens to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
115	    over $\bar k$ with explicit integrality witness, namely

152	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of prime divisor).}
153	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. A \emph{prime divisor} on $X$ is a closed integral
154	  subscheme $Y \subset X$ of codimension one. Equivalently, by the standard
155	  generic-point correspondence (Hartshorne II \S6, p.~130, ``let $\eta \in
156	  Y$ be its generic point\ldots''), $Y$ is determined by its generic point
157	  $\eta \in X$: closed integral subschemes of codimension one of $X$ are in
158	  natural bijection with points $x \in X$ such that the closure
159	  $\overline{\{x\}} \subset X$ has codimension one. The closure
160	  $\overline{\{x\}}$, equipped with the \emph{reduced induced subscheme
161	  structure}, is automatically integral (irreducible, because the closure
162	  of a single point is irreducible, and reduced by construction); the only
163	  non-automatic condition is the codimension.
164	
165	  \paragraph{Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).} The Lean structure
166	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor} bundles a point
167	  \texttt{point : X} together with a codimension-one witness, encoded via
168	  the Mathlib predicate
169	  \[
170	    \texttt{Order.coheight\ point\ =\ 1}
171	  \]
172	  on the specialisation preorder of the topological space underlying $X$.
173	  Concretely, Mathlib's preorder on \texttt{X.carrier} is defined by
174	  ``$x \leq y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x$'' (i.e.\ $x \in \overline{\{y\}}$;
175	  see \texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Specialization} and
176	  \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}). Under this convention the generic
177	  point of an integral scheme is the unique \emph{maximal} element of the
178	  preorder, so for the generic point $\eta_Y$ of a prime divisor $Y$ the
179	  only point strictly above $\eta_Y$ is the generic point $\eta_X$ of $X$,
180	  and the chain $\eta_Y < \eta_X$ has length one, giving
181	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ $\eta_Y$\ =\ 1}. The DVR/integrality content of
182	  ``the closure of \texttt{point} with the reduced subscheme structure is
183	  integral'' is automatic from $\overline{\{\,\texttt{point}\,\}}$ being
184	  irreducible, so the Lean structure does not need a separate integrality
185	  field. The iter-172 placeholder \texttt{isCodim1AndIntegral : True} in

256	\begin{definition}

259	[Order of a rational function at a prime divisor]
260	  \label{def:order_at_point}
261	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order}
262	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
263	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130--131 (valuation $v_Y$ of a prime
264	  % divisor; "zero/pole of $f$ along $Y$") (read from
265	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
266	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
267	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $Y$ is a prime divisor on $X$, let $\eta \in Y$ be its
268	  % generic point. Then the local ring $\mathcal O_{\eta, X}$ of $X$ at
269	  % $\eta$ is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field $K$, the
270	  % function field of $X$. We call the corresponding discrete valuation
271	  % $v_Y$ the \emph{valuation of $Y$}."
272	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let $f \in K^\ast$ be any nonzero rational function
273	  % on $X$. Then $v_Y(f)$ is an integer. If it is positive, we say $f$ has
274	  % a \emph{zero} along $Y$, of that order; if it is negative, we say $f$
275	  % has a \emph{pole} along $Y$, of order $-v_Y(f)$."
276	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation $v_Y$).}

278	  point $\eta \in Y$, the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is a discrete
279	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
280	  function field $K(X)$. For a nonzero rational function $f \in K(X)^{\times}$
281	  the \emph{order of $f$ along $Y$} is the integer
282	  \[
283	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
284	  \]

286	  $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$. If $\ord_Y(f) > 0$, $f$ has a \emph{zero of

288	  of order $-\ord_Y(f)$ along $Y$}.

292	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the $1$-dimensional
293	  scheme $C$); the local ring $\mathcal O_{C, P}$ is a discrete valuation
294	  ring by smoothness, and $\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)$ is the standard DVR
295	  valuation of $f$ at $P$.

389	\begin{theorem}
390	
391	\leanok
392	[The degree map is a group homomorphism]
393	  \label{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
394	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom}
395	  \uses{def:divisor_degree}
396	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
397	  $\bar k$. The degree map of \Cref{def:divisor_degree} is a group
398	  homomorphism
399	  \[
400	    \deg \colon \Div(C) \longrightarrow \mathbb Z,
401	    \qquad
402	    \deg(D_1 + D_2) \;=\; \deg(D_1) + \deg(D_2),
403	    \qquad
404	    \deg(-D) \;=\; -\deg(D).
405	  \]
406	\end{theorem}
407	
408	\begin{proof}
409	  \uses{def:divisor_degree}
410	  \leanok
411	  Immediate from the definition: $\Div(C)$ is the free abelian group on the
412	  set of closed points of $C$, and $\deg$ is the unique group homomorphism
413	  sending each generator $[P]$ to $1 \in \mathbb Z$ (equivalently: the sum
414	  of the multiplicities). Both additivity and negation thus reduce to the
415	  corresponding identities for finite sums of integers.
416	\end{proof}

429	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$, and let $f \in K^\ast$
430	  % be a nonzero function on $X$. Then $v_Y(f) = 0$ for all except finitely
431	  % many prime divisors $Y$."

434	  % $(f) = \sum v_Y(f) \cdot Y$, where the sum is taken over all prime

442	  many prime divisors $Y$ on $X$; hence the formal sum

454	\begin{theorem}
455	
456	\leanok
457	[The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism]
458	  \label{thm:principal_hom}
459	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom}
460	  \uses{def:principal_divisor, def:order_at_point}
461	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, the paragraph after the principal-divisor
462	  % definition, p.~131 (read from
463	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
464	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Note that if $f, g \in K^\ast$, then $(f/g) = (f) - (g)$
465	  % because of the properties of valuations. Therefore sending a function
466	  % $f$ to its divisor $(f)$ gives a homomorphism of the multiplicative
467	  % group $K^\ast$ to the additive group $\operatorname{Div} X$, and the
468	  % image, which consists of the principal divisors, is a subgroup of
469	  % $\operatorname{Div} X$."
470	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (group-homomorphism remark).}
471	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. The principal-divisor map
472	  $\div \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \Div(X)$ is a group homomorphism from the
473	  multiplicative group of nonzero rational functions to the additive group
474	  of Weil divisors. Concretely
475	  \[
476	    \div(fg) \;=\; \div(f) + \div(g),
477	    \qquad
478	    \div(f^{-1}) \;=\; -\div(f),
479	    \qquad
480	    \div(1) \;=\; 0.
481	  \]
482	\end{theorem}
483	
484	\begin{proof}
485	  \uses{def:principal_divisor, def:order_at_point}
486	  \leanok
487	  Each prime divisor $Y$ on $X$ contributes a discrete valuation $v_Y$ on
488	  $K(X)$ (\Cref{def:order_at_point}); by the standard DVR axioms,
489	  $v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)$ and $v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)$ for $f, g \in
490	  K(X)^{\times}$, and $v_Y(1) = 0$. Summing these identities over all prime
491	  divisors of $X$ (a sum that has finite support on each factor by
492	  \Cref{def:principal_divisor}, so the support of the sum is the union
493	  of the two finite supports and remains finite) gives the three displayed
494	  identities.
495	\end{proof}

497	\begin{theorem}
498	
499	\leanok
500	[Principal divisors have degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve]
501	  \label{thm:principal_deg_zero}
502	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero}
503	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom}
504	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138 (read from
505	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 155).
506	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE3.
507	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.10. \emph{A principal divisor on a complete
508	  % nonsingular curve $X$ has degree zero. Consequently the degree function
509	  % induces a surjective homomorphism $\deg \colon \operatorname{Cl} X \to
510	  % \mathbf Z$.}"
511	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138.}
512	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
513	  $\bar k$. For every nonzero rational function $f \in K(C)^{\times}$ the
514	  associated principal divisor has degree zero:
515	  \[
516	    \deg(\div(f)) \;=\; 0 \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
517	  \]
518	\end{theorem}
519	
520	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Let $f \in K(X)^\ast$. If $f \in k$, then
521	% $(f) = 0$, so there is nothing to prove. If $f \notin k$, then the
522	% inclusion of fields $k(f) \subset K(X)$ induces a finite morphism
523	% $\varphi \colon X \to \mathbf P^1$. It is a morphism by (I, 6.12). We
524	% have $(f) = \varphi^\ast(\{0\} - \{\infty\})$. Since $\{0\} - \{\infty\}$
525	% is a divisor of degree 0 on $\mathbf P^1$, we conclude that $(f)$ has
526	% degree 0 on $X$."
527	\begin{proof}
528	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom}
529	  Following Hartshorne's Corollary~6.10. If $f \in \bar k \subset K(C)$ is
530	  a constant scalar, then $f$ has no zeros or poles, so $\div(f) = 0$ and
531	  $\deg(\div(f)) = 0$. Otherwise the inclusion of fields
532	  $\bar k(f) \subset K(C)$ exhibits $K(C)$ as a finite extension of the
533	  rational function field $\bar k(f) \cong \bar k(t)$, so the corresponding
534	  morphism $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ is finite (by the
535	  smooth-proper-curve / function-field correspondence on which a curve is
536	  determined by its function field, classical I.6.12). The principal
537	  divisor $\div(f)$ equals the pullback $\varphi^{*}([0] - [\infty])$
538	  along $\varphi$ of the degree-$0$ divisor $[0] - [\infty]$ on
539	  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$; since pullback along a finite morphism
540	  multiplies degrees by $\deg(\varphi)$, we conclude
541	  $\deg(\div(f)) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg([0] - [\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)
542	  \cdot 0 = 0$.

546	  and ``pullback along a finite morphism of curves multiplies degrees by
547	  the degree of the morphism'' (Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9, the
548	  multiplicativity of degree) are themselves separable sub-lemmas of
549	  \texttt{RR.1}; they are stated here for completeness and slotted to be
550	  closed in a follow-up iteration. The signature
551	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal\_degree\_zero}
552	  is recorded as the public face of this theorem and is depended on by
553	  the \texttt{RR.3}/\texttt{RR.4} chain.
554	\end{proof}

556	\section{Divisor class group}
557	
558	We follow Hartshorne II.6 (pp.~131--132) and define the divisor class group
559	of a curve as the cokernel of the principal-divisor homomorphism. This is
560	the \emph{Picard group} of $C$ in the curve-specific sense; the line-bundle
561	interpretation $\Cl(C) \cong \Pic(C)$ requires the construction of
562	$\mathcal O_C(D)$, which lives in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3} and is
563	out of scope here.

565	\begin{definition}
566	
567	\leanok
568	[Linear equivalence of divisors]
569	  \label{def:linear_equivalence}
570	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.LinearEquivalence}
571	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom}
572	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~131 (definition of linear equivalence
573	  % and of $\operatorname{Cl} X$) (read from
574	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
575	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$. Two divisors $D$
576	  % and $D'$ are said to be \emph{linearly equivalent}, written $D \sim D'$,
577	  % if $D - D'$ is a principal divisor. The group $\operatorname{Div} X$ of
578	  % all divisors divided by the subgroup of principal divisors is called
579	  % the \emph{divisor class group} of $X$, and is denoted by
580	  % $\operatorname{Cl} X$."
581	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (linear equivalence and
582	  $\operatorname{Cl} X$).}
583	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. Two Weil divisors $D, D' \in \Div(X)$ are
584	  \emph{linearly equivalent}, written $D \sim D'$, if and only if there
585	  exists $f \in K(X)^{\times}$ with
586	  \[
587	    D - D' \;=\; \div(f) \;\in\; \Div(X).
588	  \]
589	  The relation $\sim$ is an equivalence relation on $\Div(X)$ (the
590	  reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity all transport from the analogous
591	  identities $\div(1) = 0$, $\div(f^{-1}) = -\div(f)$, $\div(fg) =
592	  \div(f) + \div(g)$ via \Cref{thm:principal_hom}); the corresponding
593	  quotient group $\Cl(X) := \Div(X) / \mathrm{im}(\div)$ is the
594	  \emph{divisor class group} of $X$. On a smooth proper curve $C$ over
595	  $\bar k$, \Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero} implies that the degree map
596	  $\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z$ descends to a homomorphism
597	  $\deg \colon \Cl(C) \to \mathbb Z$.
598	\end{definition}

72	selects the generic point of the closed integral subscheme, and the predicate

82	The codimension-one witness is the predicate `Order.coheight point = 1` on the
83	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier` (Mathlib's convention is
84	`x ≤ y ↔ y ⤳ x`, so the generic point is the unique maximal element and a
85	generic point of a codim-1 closed integral subscheme has a length-one chain
86	above it). The closure `{point}̄` is automatically irreducible (so the reduced
87	induced subscheme structure is automatically integral); hence no separate
88	integrality field is needed.

302	
303	/-- **Linear equivalence of Weil divisors.**
304	
305	Two Weil divisors `D, D' ∈ Div(X)` are linearly equivalent, written `D ~ D'`, if
306	and only if there exists a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` with
307	`D - D' = div(f) ∈ Div(X)`.

309	`~` is an equivalence relation (reflexivity from `div(1) = 0`, symmetry from
310	`div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, transitivity from `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, all via
311	`thm:principal_hom`). The quotient `Cl(X) := Div(X) / im(div)` is the
312	**divisor class group** of `X`.

315	degree map descends to `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ`.

319	  ∃ (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0), D - D' = principal f hf

  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint} accepts a
  scheme \texttt{C : Scheme.\{u\}}, a point \texttt{P : C}, and a

  codim-$1$ regime:
  \[
    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP\ :=\
      \begin{cases}
        \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1 &
          \text{if } h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1,\\

      \end{cases}

  On the off-branch --- where \texttt{P} is not a codimension-one point
  (e.g.\ \texttt{P} is the generic point of \texttt{C}, or a closed point
  of codimension $\geq 2$ on a higher-dimensional scheme) ---
  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} returns the zero divisor $0 \in \Div(C)$. This
  convention lets the Lean signature avoid threading
  \texttt{[OneDimensional C]} / \texttt{[IsIntegral C]} typeclass payload
  at the declaration head; the well-definedness consumer must instead
  invoke the bridge equation lemmas
  \Cref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single} and \Cref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
  to expose the two branches at the point of use.

  On the intended regime (one-dimensional integral scheme $C$, $P$ a
  closed point), the chapter's mathematical statement
  $[P] := 1 \cdot P \in \Div(C)$ is recovered by combining
  \Cref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single} with the chain
  $\texttt{IsClosed \{P\}} \Rightarrow \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1$
  that the prover threads inside the consumer (using the
  one-dimensional-integrality content).

  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single}

  \uses{def:divisor_closed_point, def:prime_divisor}

  and $P \in C$ is a closed point, then $P$ is automatically a prime
  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the $1$-dimensional
  scheme $C$); the local ring $\mathcal O_{C, P}$ is a discrete valuation
  ring by smoothness, and $\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)$ is the standard DVR
  valuation of $f$ at $P$.

  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-175 pin, per analogist
  \texttt{dvr-rationalmap-order}).} The iter-175 Lean signature is
  \begin{quote}
    \texttt{noncomputable def order \{X : Scheme.\{u\}\} [IsIntegral X]\\
    \phantom{}\quad[IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)\\
    \phantom{}\quad[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]\\
    \phantom{}\quad(f : X.functionField) : $\mathbb Z$}
  \end{quote}
  with \emph{no} $f \neq 0$ hypothesis (junk-on-$f = 0$ convention,
  see below). The body is
  \[
    \texttt{order}\ Y\ f \;:=\;
      \texttt{WithZero.log}\ \bigl(
        \texttt{Ring.ordFrac}\ (X.\texttt{presheaf.stalk}\ Y.\texttt{point})\ f
      \bigr).
  \]
  \emph{Mathlib API path.}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item \texttt{Ring.ordFrac R : K $\to\!\!*_0$ $\mathbb Z^{m_0}$}
      (with $\mathbb Z^{m_0} = \texttt{WithZero (Multiplicative}\ \mathbb Z)$),
      from \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic} (Stacks
      tag~\texttt{02MD}), is Mathlib's canonical monoid-with-zero hom
      extending the length-of-quotient valuation
      \texttt{Ring.ord} from a Noetherian Krull-dim-$\leq 1$ ring to
      its fraction field via \texttt{Submonoid.LocalizationMap.lift\textsubscript{0}}.
      For a DVR-uniformised local ring its value on $r \in R$ is the
      length of $R/(r)$ (the DVR valuation); on $a/b \in K$, it is
      $\texttt{ord}(a) - \texttt{ord}(b)$. The hypotheses
      \texttt{[CommRing R] [Nontrivial R] [IsNoetherianRing R]\\
      [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]} are exactly the typeclasses the body
      synthesises from $[\texttt{IsIntegral}\ X]$ (delivers
      \texttt{IsDomain} hence \texttt{Nontrivial} on the stalk via
      \texttt{instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral},
      and \texttt{IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
      X.functionField} via
      \texttt{instIsFractionRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafFunctionField}),
      from $[\texttt{IsLocallyNoetherian}\ X]$ (delivers
      \texttt{IsNoetherianRing} on the stalk via
      \texttt{instIsNoetherianRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsLocallyNoetherian}),
      and from the explicitly threaded
      \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]}.
    \item \texttt{WithZero.log : $\mathbb Z^{m_0} \to \mathbb Z$}
      from \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero} is Mathlib's
      canonical projection to the additive value group with
      junk-on-zero: \texttt{log\_zero}: $\log 0 = 0$;
      \texttt{log\_mul}: $\log(x \cdot y) = \log x + \log y$ for
      $x, y \neq 0$. This makes the downstream additive identities
      $v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)$, $v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)$,
      $v_Y(1) = 0$ available as named lemmas for
      \Cref{thm:principal_hom}.
  \end{itemize}

  \emph{Junk-on-$f = 0$ convention.} Setting $f = 0$ in
  \texttt{X.functionField} feeds $0$ into
  \texttt{Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)} (zero of the
  monoid-with-zero hom; \texttt{Ring.ordFrac} maps $0$ to
  $0 \in \mathbb Z^{m_0}$); then \texttt{WithZero.log 0 = 0} so
  $\texttt{order}\ Y\ 0 = 0$. This is the canonical Mathlib junk
  choice; no separate $f \neq 0$ hypothesis is threaded on the
  signature.

  \emph{In-tree gap-fill (project-side; Mathlib upstream opportunity).}
  Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) has no direct bridge from the
  topological condition $\texttt{Order.coheight}\ Y.\texttt{point} = 1$
  on \texttt{X.carrier} to the algebraic condition
  $\texttt{Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)}$ on the
  stalk. The missing fact (Stacks tag~\texttt{02IZ} / \texttt{005X}) is
  the topological identity ``coheight of $x$ in the scheme's
  specialisation preorder equals the height of the prime ideal
  corresponding to $x$ in any affine chart''; the algebraic step
  ``ideal height $=$ stalk Krull dim'' is in Mathlib as
  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height}) and
  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}). The iter-175
  workaround is to thread the explicit instance
  \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} on
  \texttt{order} (and downstream consumers); a future two-line
  Mathlib upstream PR pinning the coheight-height bridge would let
  the project drop the explicit argument in favour of synthesis from
  \texttt{coheight Y.point = 1} via the \texttt{PrimeDivisor} carrier.
\end{definition}

# Blueprint Writer Report

## Slug
weildivisor-doc-updates

## Status
COMPLETE

## Target chapter
blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex

## Changes Made

- **Refactored "Lean signature scope" paragraph** on `def:divisor_closed_point` (L412–L446
  of the post-edit chapter). Replaced the old plan-style paragraph (which described a
  "typeclass-threaded promotion in the proof body" that the iter-174 Lean did not
  take) with the actual iter-174 junk-branch convention:
  - Signature `(P : C) (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C))`.
  - Definition `if h : Order.coheight P = 1 then Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 else 0`
    (typeset as a `\begin{cases}` block).
  - Off-branch returns `0 ∈ Div(C)` (called out for the generic-point /
    higher-codim cases).
